speaker: FRANK ROWAN
words: One time,and his wife left him. He was, a rustler, you know, (laughs) His father-in-law went to work on my crew, work for the company. He said,"You know, quit that son of a bitch and come home, you know what he done? He come right over there too!"That old guy every time he'd tell that, he'd swear.

speaker: LOTTIE ROWAN
words: Well that's alright. He let her stay home.

speaker: FR
words: I would have told him, you ve got a pair of shoes there, you su|»e can handle him.(laughs) He didn't say a word. He collected all of our and drove off. (laughs)

speaker: SAM SCHRAGER
words: I wanted to ask you about Hays getting shot. What you heard about that.

speaker: FR
words: He was a policeman.

speaker: SS
words: What did you hear about that?

speaker: FR
words: Well, it was all hearsay what I got. But the thing is, both men swore they'd kill each other.They had a grievance against each other. Both hotheaded. So the story is that Hays went over there.They were enjoying themselves, a little family scrap, you know. It was habitual with them And Hayes come over there. And so she told that she met him at the door and told him to go away, that they didn't want him there. He burst on in anyway..Rifle bullet cut his jugular vein. He emptied his six shooter in the house and then made it aback as far as the railroad track there at the depot and died there. Theh Hays boys, the begun shooting up there at Sly's house and of course Sly, he was shooting back. Aid there used to be that siii board on the hotel, somebody's bullets were in that sign that were shooting at the Hayes boys, I don't know if they're still there or not.

speaker: SS
words: I don't think the sign's there anymore.

speaker: FR
words: Possibly not.

speaker: LR
words: Not in all this years. That was way over, that go 'fore my time there and we're married way over fifty years. Sixty years or so you know.

speaker: SS
words: What,they got him to give up, Sly?

speaker: FR
words: Yes. He was down there couple three years in Boise. Got married down there and learned a trade while he was in the penitentury. He never came back except to

speaker: SS
words: I heard a story that Hays had some friends that wanted to lynch Sly. Did you ever hear that?

speaker: FR
words: Wanted to do what?

speaker: SS
words: Lynch Sly.

speaker: FR
words: I hadn't heard that. And I wasn't a member there that time. I was afterwards (Oddfellows) But I don't think there was any members in it that would be inclined to do that, no.

speaker: SS
words: Hays, he wasn't very well liked?

speaker: FR
words: Well,in a sense, no, he wasn't a popular man. He was a good marshall, alright. Hits Week he done it. Buthe wasn't very popular. His boys wasn't either. He was honest, the boys crooks. Poker players and crooks. One fellow farmed. Farms just this side of the Burnt Ridge cemetery there. The road goes right through by the old house down at the end of the road, he had a farm. Then this Henry played poker and run a barbershop in town there.

speaker: SS
words: Why weren't they liked?

speaker: FR
words: They were dirty, underhanded sort of guys. The old man was, but he was honest. He wasn't liked, but he was honest. And above board with everything.

speaker: SS
words: Why was it that they didn't like Hays? 'Cause he was too hard on people?

speaker: FR
words: Well, I don't know. I heard people, other people complain that if they were disagreeing with the law he'd play it straight on 'em pretty quick, but I guess if you didn't he'd let you alone. I never heard of him imposing on anybody that way. Ihere was one fellow, he ordered him out of town. I don't know, Thil got kind of sassy about it and he got on the horse and I guess Hays told him he'd shoot him if he didn't get out of town.And he says,"Old man, don't you for a damn minute ever try that." And he roped him.

speaker: SS
words: Did he rope Hays?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah, he probably would too. I don't think that Hays was afraid of him. Because that kind of a man doesn't scare, you know.If he'd really been afraid of him or if he really wanted to , but it must he that he had some regards for the guy. Didn't want to mess with him. And time Nigger Joe, he ordered him out of town. Well Nigger, he got on his horse and went, but he took out the sidewalk. And he was shooting at the son of a gun too. Joe, he didn't come back to town for quite a while. There wasn't one of them that wasn't scared of him. For gods sake, can't hit a nigger in the dark.(laughs) He had And the fella on the horse just broke through there, broke his leg and maybe broke the nigger's neck. Course, a drunken man doesn't give too many things consideration. I wanted to mention another case. Here, a few years ago a fella come up from Lewiston, I guess he was hunting, I don't know, but he had a high power rifle with him, he'd go down a steep hill to sight a deer beyond Bear there. Over in there, he was hunting over in there and he was entertaining himself, I suppose, shooting off these insulators on the power line. The damn line broke and burned him right there.

speaker: SS
words: Killed him?

speaker: FR
words: That, you don't know abot the  do you?

speaker: SS
words: I don't-think I do.

speaker: FR
words: There'd be several people, anybody there, this is recent,

speaker: LR
words: That guy wasn't hunting. He was just going along the road...

speaker: FR
words: He must have come hunting. He wouldn't come up to shoot insulators. He could do that down there in town. He must have been hunting.But.

speaker: SS
words: Sort of came back on him?

speaker: FR
words: Just under the son of a gun, you see.

speaker: LR
words: Flew around and come onto him and cooked him.He was having a lot of fun of shooting insulators out.

speaker: FR
words: There's any amount of people in Deary that would recollect that,

speaker: SS
words: Do they know that it was Sly that shot Hays? getting up on that sidewalk it was just showing contempt for the marshall.

speaker: FR
words: YEah.

speaker: SS
words: They're sure that he did it?

speaker: FR
words: He was shot in that house, yeh.Some say she done it. Course, she was there and talked to the marshall. Cut his jugular veins.The Slys killed him alright, whichever one done it.

speaker: LR
words: He was asking for it. He had no business after he was ordered not to come in there if they didn't senA for him. He had no business cutting in there.

speaker: FR
words: Then she tell him to keep away.^She did. Course, that's their story too. What ever talk went on between them, nobody knows. Except them.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that she tried to slip Sly a gun. When he was waiting to be taken to Moscow.

speaker: FR
words: I heard that he was killed right in the door. Just as he entered the door.

speaker: SS
words: I mean, when Sly was in jail. Before they took him to Moscow.

speaker: FR
words: That would be possible. If she could get to the jail.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that the Hays boys were not liked at all,

speaker: FR
words: They were dirty. Crooked in poker and mean.

speaker: SS
words: Did they stay around for long after Hays...?

speaker: FR
words: Oh yeah, long time afterwards. I don't know what the reason was. I guess they were homesteading. One was out somewheres in the dust country and then another fella wound up in Tacoma I think. But granny, she stayed there til she died. She used to be in town there, walking around with a stick.

speaker: LR
words: I guess I must have seen her, but seems to me like she was quite a heavy woman.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that the daughter went bad.

speaker: FR
words: I've heard that too. She married a dentist. And then they split up. I heard that. I don't know anything about it.I've seen here, couple times, often but I saw her a couple times. She was a good looking woman, too.

speaker: LR
words: Some of thase,oldtimers, I can see 'em as if they're standing before me right now. I can remember so well. And others I can't remember. Course, I never her as much as the other people. Some of them I just don't..

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever see Joe Wells? Did you know him at all?

speaker: LR
words: No. I've seen, the negro you mean? I've seen him, yeah. I heard him talk or anything like that. Just see him.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know him at all, Frank?

speaker: FR
words: By sight, that's all. I never got acquainted. I think the feeling Joe, course he didn't have a whole lot  do with it, he was dead, but a bootlegger come into Troy lumber camp up there. And he had a few gallons of liquor with him and of course he unloaded it there. And the nigger had died a week or a month before that time. The word got around, somebody had a joke that this had killed the nigger. Couple of boys from camp, they drank a little bit too much and they got sick from it. They come in on Saturday. On Monday these fellas didn't go out to work. They were sitting there after supper and this one fella, Carl Johnson, hk$ family's on Texas Ridge today, he was sitting there and he looked over at the other guy and he said,"Well you know, they say he killed a nigger.And he came goddamn near to me too."(laughs) I thought that was about as well timed as...(laughs)

speaker: LR
words: What do you think of putting up with that noise and paying over five hundred dollars for it? Five fifty (discussion about heaters) We moved off of that into town.Before we finally after a few months moved up to Helmer, Thats when they had passenger trains. I don't know, how many trains a day was it? They had the freight and passenger trains, the trains going and going every little while.

speaker: FR
words: You mean at Troy?

speaker: LR
words: Yeah.

speaker: FR
words: That was two passenger tcains both ways, daily

speaker: LR
words: And then the frieght.And especially at night, you know the freight, well there would be the passenger too, at night.

speaker: SS
words: Noisy?

speaker: IR
words: It was awful noisy. And we knew that because it was noisy going up the canyon, we could hear it clear on up before it got up on top. We was still on the ranch. And so we thought, oh, that would be awful, that awful noise down there. And people said,"That won't bother, for a week or so. You'll never hear it." And couldn't believe that, you know. There was no time that we never heard the train. People that lived down along the railroad tracks said,"It was that way with us at first. We thought we never can live here, that noise going on all the time. Trains and whistles going on.Now it doesn't bother us a bit. We never hear 'em" And we wasn't along th^track. We was pretty much up.the hill. But it was plenty noisy but it was no time it didn't bother us. You get used to things.

speaker: SS
words: Did that make Troy a lively town, having the train in there?

speaker: FR
words: No different. What put the life into Troy in the first place was when that Potlatch railroad went in over there and thfch another thing helped it the same time, thidbig -|r6Hc used to be here west of Troy, it's a fill now. It's right where you pass the log cabin on your left coming to Moscow. It's over to the right and there was a big trestle there. They there for six months filling that. Put that cut in. That's up now at the underpass. "The railroad went around the hill there..Put in that cut and made it for fill down there.

speaker: SS
words: There's a lot of work in doing that?

speaker: FR
words: There was a big crew there and two work teams there all winter. And then they had for water they had the pipeline laid way behind the over onto that creek. Must have been a couple miles or more away. Speaking of Moscow, was wanting to get the Potlatch mill years ago. Why, the devil would they do for water?

speaker: SS
words: No water here. .

speaker: FR
words: No water here for that. That shows you how little they knew about that The same thing as that Weippe before the Lewiston mill went in. The mill was going into Weippe up there. Well they didn't have enough water to do their laundry up there. They were so sure of it andAdouble camp was put in there.

speaker: LR
words: Talking about if railroads make a town, I can tell you one of it. When we were going to school we lived in a little town ten miles out of Grangeville, about ten miles out of Cottonwood and moved back between the two. Named Denver. And it was quite a little town. Lot of people lived there and a lot of business places. Even had a bank.

speaker: FR
words: Three big hotels, saloon.

speaker: LR
words: Poolhall and they had this saloon. Post office.

speaker: FR
words: Chumches.

speaker: LR
words: Little restuarants and churches. And it was quite a booming little place,

speaker: FR
words: And it had a water works, city water works.

speaker: LR
words: Lot of people lived there. Well, they were coming in with the railroad from Lewiston up, come across the prajrie, didn't have any railroad up at that time, had to freight by horse and wagon. And wasn't trucks in\those days either. And at that time nobody had cars, so it was horses. And so then this, they were going to build a railroad in. Well they bringing it ghrough Denver there. And that might have done something for other little towns.But that's when they changed, by-passed it, went over north of, anyway, four miles awau from Denver. Well, poor little Denver just died out finally. But you know, and that was a real nice place to stay in, which is still there,what there is of it. Railroad station like. And a real nice place to build a town, but it never built up at all. It just didn't...

speaker: SS
words: It was off the beaten path.

speaker: LR
words: Well they put the railroad right through. But they didn't take it through Denver. If they'd put that railroad through Denver it probably would have soon been a pretty good little city.

speaker: FR
words: I don't know. YOu see, Cottonwood never made too much of a town. That was the trouWle with Denver. Cottonwood was closer and Gragneville was here.

speaker: SS
words: Was that country, how did it compare to this country.

speaker: LR
words: Just the same.Yes. Lots of and lots of snow and lots of wind blowing.

speaker: FR
words: Really milder winters there than here, thougl^jup on the Camas Prairie.The winters are really milder because lots of times you'd have bare grounds there where we didn't begin to, I'm talking about Troy,not here.

speaker: LR
words: It's very much the same. To me it made no difference when I come to Troy.

speaker: SS
words: Was that country settled up as this country was?

speaker: LR
words: Yeah. Yeah, just about the same thing. Yes, it was at that time. They've combined ranches there and now there's just a few real large ranches and there isn't so many homes there. Across the prairie. It's a big prairie just like really this is a big prairie. Stre iches out each way and then makes a big prairie. Our oldest daughter in Montana called the other day, we were talking about we figured how the difference the weather was then we'd say well they compare pretty well, Grangeville and here. The praries would come out about the same I guess. Snow and winds and drifting so it comes out the same thing.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the winters were a lot harder then?

speaker: LR
words: Oh I should say so! There on the ranch, we'd just be drifted in solid. And then when we'd get plowed out, it has happened, maybe the next day or two or three days or a week, it's the same old thing, we just in there solid. Oh we hate to see the winds come up when it was snowing.

speaker: FR
words: You betcha!

speaker: LR
words: It was terrible. Our oldest daughter, you talk snow to her, she's ready to fight! She just hates the snow. She had, poor thing,, such a hard going to school in that hard winters, you know. So much snow to wade. And so nucy much of the time Frank was in the woods, not to even help her make a trail out to the highways. So she had a hard time of it. And she just hates winter!

speaker: SS
words: I guess it's a lot tougher. It doesn't seem so bad now.

speaker: LR
words: Oh no, it isn't!

speaker: FR
words: She made a remark there awhile ago that reminded me of aremark that one of her relatives from Arkansas made years ago. They was talking about, came in -fe I was in there, that was in Moscow Hotel. Mrs. Owen Zaner come in there. They was talking different things about the weather and she,"Well the only difference about Arkansas is out here you leave your tracks.In Arkansas it all went with you." No the difference was in Arkansas you left your tracks but here you took 'em with you. That just kept getting thicker, The 'dobe, you left your tracks in Arkansas, but here you took 'em with you. That 'dobe up there keeps sticking, getting thicker.

speaker: SS
words: What?

speaker: FR
words: That 'dobe laying on the Camas Prairie. Lots of times you had to unlock the brake locks out of your wagon, wheels would build up.

speaker: LR
words: When it would come those deep snows wind blowing and drifting like that and it just drift the railroad track in, trains couldnt get through..

speaker: FR
words: Week at a time.

speaker: LR
words: There'd be sometimes two weeks they'd have to bring the cows up from Lewiston to keep aplowing through. And then they'd get up there, that's where we seen 'em working there. . And on when they'd go towards Grangeville and they d have a big something going there^really cutting and throwing that snow both ways.They'd be without train service there for days.

speaker: SS
words: Did that happen around here too? In Troy?

speaker: LR
words: I don't think the trains was ever stuck, was they?

speaker: FR
words: No, not on the Troy branch unless there was a derailment or something.

speaker: SS
words: How did you two meet?

speaker: LR
words: That's a sad story..Don't go into that.

speaker: SS
words: Sad story?

speaker: LR
words: Don't you see?(laughs)

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like a sucess story to me.

speaker: LR
words: The second year he had worked for my aunt..

speaker: FR
words: Harvest.

speaker: LR
words: and her husband...

speaker: FR
words: I was up there harvestime.

speaker: LR
words: Out on the prairie. And the second year he was working there well then, she had little twin girls and then she had a younger girl. She had three babies and doing all the work herself, and they got me down there to help her. That's what happened.'Cause all the guys was there and I had to that. There was four or five other men working there. That was the harvest time.

speaker: FR
words: Then her sister came down to our place where our oldest kid was born, and she married Jesse Spencer's brother. That was the first house on the left as you go out of Troy. Mile and a quarter out of town.

speaker: LR
words: Herb Spencer.

speaker: FR
words: One of his sons has the trailer house in the woods just a ways. And another son lives out in there and a third son lives in the second house on the road on the left hand side there.

speaker: LR
words: The first house on the right. No, they are on the left.(both talk at once) I'd be alright going towards Deary, until I got up there almost to where this one son lives now where his grandmother lives.(pause)

speaker: SS
words: Who?

speaker: FR
words: The fellow that used to live at Troy, he's dead now.He went back to Fargo, North Dakota, he's got a son there. He's in the U.S. employment office. So he bought a deep freeze at a bargain there, had it in his pickup and he was coming west. He come over a hill, he was doing about sixty, wasn't too much of a hill in that Dakota country. But there was some new oil there. He said boy he'd done some careful driving. Easing up on that throttle for a while, pretty slick when it's new.

speaker: SS
words: you ever hear the story about Driscoll getting stabbed in Troy?

speaker: FR
words: Oh yeah, that was known as the Driscolls there. He was a stranger. Yeah.He come down from the Oddfellows hall there, what was that fellow's name?

speaker: SS
words: I've heard it was Shoemaker.

speaker: FR
words: Tiidkri Shoemaker, yeah. There's a lot of stories that afterwards. Driscoll was a Pullman, none of those Driscolls from Troy. But he was a mouthy, he might not been a trouble maker but he was awful mouthy. He had a pretty good jag on him up there. And this girl, he was bothering her up there.So Shoemaker stepped in and told him to let her alone. Mind his own business. That was alright, he did.He took Shoemaker's word for it, I guess. And then Tilden started, after the show, started down to that Oddfellows Hall, where would you say it is today? Right, you know where the lumber outfit's rigged up out there? Well that building across there, apartments, that was the hall. So Tilden was going down. This guy got in close behind him and he making remarks about Tilden. Said something about he was afraid of him and talking like that. And Tilden stopped, and turned around and said,"Are you talking to me?" And Driscoll said something and he got the knife and stuck it in his side. Shoemaker said that he put his hand back like he was reaching for a gun and some people said that he had a gun. And there was no gun found on him though when he was dead. He run down to the old post office building, there was a couple doctors upstairs.He says, "My god, who'll care for mother now?" he says. He crumpled down the sidewalk and died.Some said...

speaker: LR
words: Was that Driscoll?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Some said?

speaker: FR
words: Some said that Driscoll was kind of making up to this girl and you can't tell, you hear so many stories.

speaker: SS
words: But Shoemaker stabbed him in the sides?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah, Shoemaker was making up to this girl.And he stuck the knife in him and he didn't bother him anymore. He had to be a troublemaker getting fresh up there with the girls and then following him.

speaker: SS
words: Did you know Shoemaker?

speaker: FR
words: Oh hell, I knew Shoemaker very well.

speaker: LR
words: That wouldn't be any of Oscar's, would it?

speaker: FR
words: No, he was, his father's up therein that Spencer cemetery of ours. No, he was from Kentucky.

speaker: LR
words: That was kind of like a case last summer or fall up at Stites that that guy resigned...

speaker: FR
words: Yeah, Stites, that policeman.

speaker: LR
words: He resigned from the police force and then a few hours after he shot and killed a guy there in town. And so we read all about it our grand daughter and her husband were up here in the winter and he kind of laughed and said,"You know the only thing about that,A should have been done years ago." He says,"That guy was absolutely no good. He was always looking for trouble, picking trouble. He finally got it, but he didn't get it soon enough."

speaker: FR
words: The police...

speaker: LR
words: He said,"That man is not to blamed. He was picking on him and always looking for trouble. That's his life. He won't do it anymore now."

speaker: SS
words: This was the man who was killed?

speaker: LR
words: Uh huh. He said he was no good.

speaker: FR
words: Troublemaker. Always looking for trouble.

speaker: LR
words: Said he was nothing but a trouble maker.

speaker: FR
words: ...about Shoemaker and I knew him for a couple of yars. We both belonged to the Elks Club and naturally got pretty well acquainted. But I couldn't see any reason or anything mean about Shoemaker that way. Some of 'em said "fell that don't mean they're all that way,

speaker: SS
words: What's the story on Clemm getting killed?

speaker: FR
words: He had a resturant in there. No, nigger had the resturant, who was he, I believed he worked in there. Maybe he cooked. Anyway this,time of this activity going on there, the railroad over here at Deary and the field, couple niggers come in there, it was quite late and they put in a resturant right where Jimmy Arnett's Fonks' is now.And of course, there'd be some girls come up from Lewiston occas donally. There was some of them there one night in the resturant and the next morning Clemm was dead out on the side walk. They arrested the nigger; for it. I don't know what he got. He didn't get the death penalty or anything, but he got quite a sentence. And the saloon keeper that took it up, he didn't think he got quite a deal, he got another trial, he him. He done a good job for the town.That damn Clemm was no good.

speaker: SS
words: He cleared the negroes?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that Clemm drank a lot and was a troublemaker.

speaker: FR
words: I don't know if he was much of a troublemaker, but he was such a useless drunk, he neglected his family. I never heard of doing any fighting. The little bastard couldn't have done. It wouldn't have amounted to anything anyhow if he did.But then, they can make an awful nusiance of themselves, regardless of the size.

speaker: SS
words: You say the girls come up from Lewiston, were they sporting girls?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah.The niggers had split up, there was only one, I think it was Squires was there at the time. The other nigger had sold up and went off, He had a good resturant, done good business there,

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how Clemm was killed?

speaker: FR
words: I guess they just hammered him up enough to get him. Probably didn't figure they were doing enough when they done it.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like Troy was pretty...

speaker: LR
words: It had it's share of excitement,

speaker: FR
words: Well, to walk the street and be around there, you wouldn't notice it. But then, when you look back over it, it's alittle different (than what it used to be. But it never appeared that way, you know, when were living there.

speaker: SS
words: I heard that in the early days, a lot of it was the lumberjacks coming in from out in the woods and spending their money.

speaker: FR
words: They never made much noise. That didn't, maybe some of the native lumberjacks did, but not the transients, not their way. It seemed like they travel and get along fine. If they do any celebrating or getting into trouble, it's after they're working and have a payday. That was the way that worked, But you never paid any attention,had any fear of lumberjacks. I never did.

speaker: SS
words: Do you buy the idea that nine out of ten lumberjacks drank up what they made?

speaker: FR
words: Quite a few of 'em did, yeah. Hell of a lot of 'em. More than you think. Men that you figured knew different too. There's splendid men among themr in several years up there. And they were as fine a men as you'd want to meet. Square with every body. But they would do that. I don't know what was the reason. Them days, they went broke and they take like in Spokane and they see another lumberjack, hit 'em up, get a little money. I tended bar, I'm moving to Canada now, I tended bar in Grand Forks, B,C, there in a hotel. The Pacific. Grand Forks was down here. Out here about a mile was the CPR station and out here about a mile was the Great Northern station, little town a lot of lumber going. And fellow come in there, go broke, going out to work, come back drink five, ten dollars, whatever he wanted. Give him a bottle, . on his way, He invariably came back and squared that, That you didn't take much of a chance it. Fellow come in there one night, he come in there and he was drunk.The boss was on shift. Well he says,"Charlie, I'm back." Maybe grouchy, sober or drunk, but anyway, he wasn't in very good humor. Says," "I'm back and I'm broke." Unsaid, pay your money. Well,"that's kind of a hell of a way to do,"he says.He called me a son of a gun. He said if he didn't like it, come on up, he'd make it alright with him.Redheaded bartender we had there. He says,"Charlie don't have to do that, sure he's under the weather a little today."Said,"Sold the devil out and forget about it."(laughs) But they do that for them fellows right along.There was one case though, where Course, he might have done it another time. But he didn't do it that time. He got drunk and broke fore he got there.

speaker: SS
words: I've heard the same thing. That most of the time you could count on a guy that if he'd borrowed something he'd pay it back. Sounds like a different breed of men thai! what I've run into.

speaker: LR
words: That was the old days.

speaker: FR
words: Seldom ever see a married man in them camps. Unless he was a foreman or a timekeeper or something. Blacksmith. Rank and file were single men.

speaker: LR
words: That's the way of a lot of little places around. If you've ever heard of Elk City back of Grangeville. Places like that, they used to be and shooting and murder and all that in all those places about the same time as Troy. Well it's not those kind of people there at all today. It's just peaceful people and you very seldom hear of anything like that. But theJJ had their rough days too.

speaker: FR
words: That Grangeville, Troy didn't have anything like Grangeville. That's just to show you, there was a Mex there, he was, I guess he was Mex with Indian. Anyway, he was part Mex. And his wife was teaching there,she was too, not a fullblood. Well he's one of these guys that get drunk and was mean and get the devil knocked out of him and then he'd come home and he'd slap up on her. So he done that a couple of times and he got the word ,Mallock was his name, he got the word, noM don't let that happen again or you'll be attended to. Damn fool, he didn't do that. Went ahead and cleaned up on her again. They took him to jail. Crowd come in there one night, wasn't even dark yet. There was an old trustee at the jail, he said,"You go on and mind your business." They come down the line. I had friends in the next cell at the time pretty well scared. He was in there at the same time.

speaker: LR
words: Don't try to laugh and talk at the same time, can't understand what you're saying. It isn't that funny.

speaker: FR
words: Anyway, he walks in he says,"Mallock we come in to square up with you," And he called them sons of bitches or something and turned over towards the wall. They loaded him down. He wouldn't have froze at all. But this poor devil, MacLean, he was in the next cell there to him. He was in there for cattle rustling. He said,"By god, I don't want to be scared like that again. 14 there was any damn luck, they'd take a shot at me."(laughs)

speaker: SS
words: Did they hang him?

speaker: FR
words: Just filled him up with lead. They shot him in the bunk.

speaker: LR
words: There's twenty men, he had twenty shots in him, they all took shot They were all going to give it to.

speaker: FR
words: Judge Steele he wrote Jan article about him in the paper about him. He said "That's the result of this thing of I'll give a man a sentence, maybe twenty years to life for murder, then 2,3 years he's out of jail again and running around. Now that's what you get for doing things like that."

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hear about them hanging Ed Hill? Over in Colfax?

speaker: FR
words: No that's out of my territory,

speaker: SS
words: A similar thing happened to him. He got hung by vigilantes.

speaker: LR
words: What was he?

speaker: SS
words: He supposedly killed a cop.Some say it was for horse stealing.

speaker: FR
words: We got very little news of that country at that time.We had the twice a week Spokesman-Review .Not very many radios and tv's around them days.

speaker: LR
words: Rustling horses, there used to be a lot of that around Whitebird and Grange ville. My dad had a horse taken out in a bunch like that one time. And as they took a bunch of horses through, past, my dad wasn't married then,.He and his mother were living together out on the prairie and they could tell it was a whole bunch of horses going and he got up and looked. But they was, one of them neighed as " went by. Dad said that he knew that that was Nellie. He knew her neigh. So he said they took her in the bunch. And do you know what that old fool done?I told him so too. I said, well you darn old fool! He signed a pardon for that guy to get out of jail! They went around with a petition, you know.this Rook I guess was his name, to get him out of jail. I guess he was the boss of everything you know, He was the one that got away with dad's horse, lot of others. And I don't know how it was that dad said that, they broughtAto him and he signed it and I said, well you darn old fool! I liked to have been there and scratched it off. I said what did you do that for!The way he was doing."Oh well," he says.

speaker: SS
words: It was a real ring?

speaker: LR
words: Yeah.

speaker: FR
words: You set your head for th«nt. The old man had his points too. He was a tax payer in Idaho and it was costing Idaho money to keep that man so maybe the old gentleman looked deeper than the ordinary people do in a thing like that,

speaker: LR
words: And his wife stuck up for him, of course.She just denied that he didn't steal horses. And his sister-in-law, was telling about it. What was her name? Matty. I'll call her Matty. And she said,"Oh you . that."and she gets so mad and tells you all about that he didn't do that, that he wasn't into that."He's a good man."

speaker: FR
words: Ue was a good man. He made money off of it. In late years he was one of the aristocrats of the Camas Prairie country. Damn right he was,

speaker: SS
words: In the early years I thought a man could be hung for that.

speaker: FR
words: Oh yes.Before Bill got big enough to straddle a horse.

speaker: LR
words: There was a man down in the Whitebird country was hung for, what did he do? They hung An the there?

speaker: FR
words: That guy that shot

speaker: SS
words: What story is that?

speaker: FR
words: Oh something about a horsethief they was after. You hear various stories on that too. Some figured that it was wrong that they bothered him. figured that he ought to be hung. But Monty Jackson I don't know much about it.

speaker: LR
words: Well the sad part of it was whatever it was, at least one family there that was with him on it. Didn't want to see it happen. So the meb was after him. All over the hills and mountains there down at Whitebird, just right down- Do you know Whitebird? Right down in a hole, you know.All around the hills. And this man give him the best saddle horse that he had to try to make his getaway from 'em. But they got him. And I don't know whether it was these people's place or not, but anyway, they got him and they hung him right out in the front yard o$ some people's home. And the woman just about went crazy about it. And he made them come and cut that tree down. The husband made 'em come and cut that tree down. To think that they would even do that. They couldn't take him out til the woods with all the trees that are around or make a scaffold or something, you know. They have to take him in their yard and hang him there.

speaker: FR
words: That kind of people mix with them too.

speaker: LR
words: I said, shoot, I would have never give him a horse to try to get away. I'd try to seeh I had some kind of place where I could push him in and hide him. Frank said, they couldn't very well do that and I aaid I'd roll him under my bed. I suppose if they were searching the house they'd look under the bed, though.

speaker: SS
words: You never know what they'd do.

speaker: LR
words: I'd just look 'em right in the eye and swear I'd never seen that man. Where did you see him? Now you get out of here or I'm liable to shoot you, I want no part of this. I couldn't have see where putting him on a horse, maybe he could get back in the bushes and hide someplace, but out on those hills on a horse? Why, that's good target.

speaker: SS
words: Have you ever run across any people who were crazy?

speaker: LR
words: No. No I haven't.

speaker: SS
words: Every once in a while in the early days, somebody turns up to be off by themselves and be crazy.

speaker: LR
words: I never knew of anyone.

speaker: FR
words: I had two experiences with a crazy man. It scared the hell out of me too. Once I was a kid, that was in the old country. A fellow there that I knew well, he lived there, he had this double barreled shotgun.I was on horseback, he was afoot."Hello Frank." He looked at me,"You son of a bitch." Something about some war or something. I didn't know who he was talking to. I figured I was up against something. I didn't know him, he just went crazy, you see. So Igot out of there alive in good shape, but^ent up to that bughouse afterwards. He just simply had too much whiskey available to him for a time and went wrong. But this just below Troy there where Clemms live. This Shorty Clemm,he's dead now. Father of that Lester Clemm, lived this side of Lester on the little house on the hill. Had the grader,left it just past their mailbo5n the ditch.He'd been in the insane asylum before. That was my idea of getting excited. So I was sitting there in the seat of the grader, waiting for the guy to get the engine battery started. He comes along and he has a butcher knife and a .22 rifle in his hand. "Goddamn you, now I've got you where I want you."(laughs)Well naturally I've heard of being nuts before. I looked at him and said something and then he^said something to us and then he'd gone off and he helped somebody butcher, you see.But by god, with that butcher knife and that rifle what else would you do but get scared?

speaker: SS
words: WHo was this?

speaker: FR
words: Shorty Clemm. He's been in the insane asylum."Goddamn you, now I've got you where I want you." he said. Sitting there unarmed.

speaker: LR
words: That was the guy, in comparison to me, he was the wild man off of Burnt Ridge.

speaker: SS
words: Sort of like the same thing. What about the time, what did you hear about the man hanging in the canyon?

speaker: FR
words: Just from the boys that buried him.They went down there, somebody reports this body hanging in the canyon there, report it in here to the sheriff. Sheriff comes down and he hires these two men Carl Peterson and Tom Christie. They were boys then, you see. Probably 18,20, maybe a little older. And he hires them to bury him and they took a half gallon of whiskey along. They went down and buried him. Now nobody knew this man, that would admit it anyway,around there. Nobody knew him, found there hung. And they went down, as Tom says,"We got drunker than hell and we buried him." They got five dollars a piece. And that was alright. A while after that sometime, by gosh, somebody happened down there and find the grave empty and open.And there was a family living down in there, I don't know who they was, but the family living, and there used to be a road down there. There's still the county road.

speaker: SS
words: In Camp's Canyon?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah, but anyway, a family living down there and they'd heard a wagon come in and go out one night. But this man had disappeared and nobody knew, that would admit it, anyhow, who he was or anything.

speaker: SS
words: Nobody knew why he was hanging there in the first place?

speaker: FR
words: There was some toughs in through there. Quite a lot of horse thieving was done in there in the early days. That was, some people had ideas ,but they wouldn't say too much for sure who they was.They wouldn't say too much about it. Even there they stold horses in the early-days, back in the woods like that. But if anybody ever found out. Johnson would come the nearest to telling you anymore about it than,of anybody there anymore. But there was a stranger found dead, buried and then exhumed and taken away. Somebody knew him alright,knew where he was.

speaker: LR
words: He probably say,"Oh shucks,I really don't know." There's a man to swear,"Oh shucks,I don't know."(laughs)

speaker: FR
words: Just like the old fellow that was killed up there somewhere's opposite the cemetery on Burnt Ridge in a log cabin there. Nobody knew who done that.Or why or where. Clarence would know as much.about that as anybody.

speaker: SS
words: Camp Canyon reminds me of the Olson place.

speaker: FR
words: O.K. Olson.

speaker: SS
words: Would you tell me the story about him?

speaker: FR
words: About what I told about the house? Well he was talking to me. He'd been in town talking to somebody. Said he was digging into me why I didn't put this house up on Burnt Ridge. He had a nice farm, 80 acres up there. You know where the machine shed is on Burnt Ridge there? Well his place cornered it from there. But O.K.i he always had a grin on him and always had something to say. He was talking to somebody and wanted to know why he didn't put the house on Burnt Ridge instead of down in the canyon.He says,"You're idea and my idea wetf two different things."So I said, why the hell didn't you tell him the truth, O.K.?"What is that?"That you're a goddamn fool. He just laughed. (laughs)Nellie'd be about, course, she was as jolly as he was.She's a pretty jolly girl. His daughter, Nellie Johanson. I wish I'd seen her when she was down.

speaker: SS
words: He was a good humored guy?

speaker: FR
words: Never made 'em any better.Another time we were widing the road on the hill above theirs, and there was lots of good sized rocks.Big ones that the cat couldn't straddle.  would O.K. happened to be on the porch out there. This fellow working wtih me, he didn't know O.K. so well.He'd roll 'em and whang! O.K. says,"Hey," and he begin to laugh." Wo, he was swell.

speaker: SS
words: What did the rock hit?

speaker: FR
words: His wire fence. Course I told him, we're going to roll a lot of rocks down, come in this road and then we'll shoot 'em out of the way. "Alright," he says.

speaker: SS
words: How did he get those initials? Were they his nickname?

speaker: FR
words: Ole K. Olson. Just like the Johnson's,E.K, Johnson, All of them had the K, in the middle there.I suppose that was Knute.

speaker: SS
words: What about Johanson, did you know him at all?

speaker: FR
words: I just knew him by sight. He didn't talk English, you know. All he could do was snore in English. Funniest man, he had a business head on him, as long as he lived here, never took on English language. Depend on somebody else.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how that brickyard got going there?

speaker: FR
words: That was his work.

speaker: LR
words: Do you mean on O.K.'s place?

speaker: SS
words: I meant the one in town.There was one on O.K,'s place too?

speaker: LR
words: In town, no, this is a little mine there, 0,K.'s was

speaker: FR
words: He's talking about Johanson.

speaker: SS
words: About the brickyard in town.

speaker: LR
words: No, he says he's talking about the brickyard in town.

speaker: FR
words: Johan son was the originator of that. He had a little, done a little brick- work at home, but, and he got some money together.He put his oneAin as a manager, other son in as a bookkeeper. Got some moeny going, hired a man, built up that brickyard. Now they, I forget the name of the fellow, I knew him but he wasn't there long. But this fellow come in there and he built it up. He put up those big kilns. Well you know you don't do that in 24 hours Built them, be long, it was two kilns with a division in^here.Well them were sons of guns to build because underneath you've got, well usually man crawls through it, you got to crawl through it to work in there. Get the sand and ashes out of there. After they got that bottom in there and the and everything built, it's a big job. Costs money. So they got this man£in who built the plant. And of course the shareholders, they could see the money going but they couldn't see no money coming in. They had to get the plant built before they could do anything. Anyway, they got mad and they fired this foreman. Decided they'd hire this other foreman, they got the Johanbo^ out of there too, they did. And this Scotchman in there, Watson, well he was bragging what he done. There were 7200 in debt when he come there. Well of course they was 7200,in debt. They do it these days, they'd be 72,000 in debt. Well all he had to do was^make his brick and burn and sell 'em. He was bragging what he done. I think if that Swede had another year at her, might have been abetter than Watson did.But it satisfied them, they got out of it.Goymoney out of it alright. But Idon't think he ever had achance.He'd tell around he was no good that Johanneson hired him because he talked Swede.

speaker: FR
words: Then that Scotchman that they hired come in there,Watson, the brickmaker, he got in apile of michief.This Tom Christie, secretary of the Oddfellows Lodge, one of the fellows that buried the man in the canyon called me in one day, said,"I got ahell of aletter today'.I was working at the brickyard and there was stockholders in the thing. Itook it and read it said there was a lodge meeting in Vacouver B,C. awhile ago and he got talking to a fellow there, says, "I used to know a J.P. Watson, is that any relation of yours?" Said,"I think a very close relative." Well then he goes to read the rest to the secretary of the Oddfellows Lodge in Troy, that was this Chrisie who got this letter. So Christie said,"What do you make of that?" Went on to tell^about So many years ago my father left my mother and my and my two sisters. He either disappeared we thought, or dead.So he wrote to he wrote to Dayton*Washington before he'd been a manager, that's where this other man met him.And they told him that he went to Troy, Idaho and then he writes the secretary of the lodge there.So he wrote another letter and said something about it and says he'd like to have, if he could get a specimen of his handwriting, says,"He writes like my uncle used to write."And just as it happened, had an order in my ' just tore a sheet of paper, had an order in my or something they wanted out of the stores. Hell,I'11 send them that. I know what to get. Sent it back to him and my gosh, in a few days, the young fellow, he come along there.He come to town and he hunted up Christie, Him and Christie were walking up there. He was taking the young fellow up to the plant there and they met him. So the young fellow said something like,"Hello dad," or something like that. And it took him by surprise. He looked at him good, finally he says,"Prove it,prove it." And then before he could make any reply he said, "Is your mother and sister still alive?"

speaker: SS
words: You mean his wife left him?

speaker: FR
words: He was really a bigamist, you see. The other woman was alive in Scotland. And her sister where she stayed.

speaker: SS
words: His wife's sister?

speaker: LR
words: But you didn't tell quite all that, you know. The old man and this boy, they got together, father and son.

speaker: FR
words: They made up.

speaker: LR
words: Then they wanted to get from town something.

speaker: FR
words: Letters that the kid wrote him.

speaker: LR
words: Then they had something to come back on Christie, Christie said,"Oh no, that's mine."

speaker: FR
words: Yeah he took in big with the old man and kind of cold shouldered Christie, He wanted to get these letters, said,"You got letters I wrote you?" "Yeah." Says,"I'd like to have 'em." "You go to hell."Said, "if you what kind of comeback could I have?"

speaker: SS
words: So the kid forgot about his mother and sister in Scotland?

speaker: LR
words: Yeah.He'd found papa and they probably out how they could sue Christie and the Lodge

speaker: FR
words: Oh boy, the old man could have gone after Christie if he could.

speaker: SS
words: Why was he mad at Christie? Because Christie had put the son on to him?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah. Just because Christie wrote a truthful answer to his letters is all. Is all the harm he done. Him and Christie didn't get along anyhow.Christie was a stockholder and he didn't like him.Somebody talking one time said, "Damn funny how Rowan stays up there and gets along so good."Martin says, "Don't you know how he gets along up there? Well everytime Watson tomes near him he starts swearing. He was a rough old Scotchman, he was quite a fella to dig you, but if you dig him back, he was alright. But if you kind of get away from him, boy he'd really ride you.I was coming in one day, I brought something into the mill, it was cold.

speaker: FR
words: And as I was coming out, the fella that was attending the kiln said,"Would you help me carry that doorvin there?" So I went back in, the door, and I was coming out, the old man was coming along and he was looking over his glasses. "And who's this coming with his white gloves on?"he says. I said it's me."Who's me?"By god you hit me, you'll find out.So he started to walk off then,"Well can't you work enough to keep warm without coming in here to warm in the kilns?" By god Mr. Watson, I got plenty good clothes I don't have to work to keep warm.

speaker: FR
words: If you dig back at him like that, why it was alright.(laughs) You see a fellow one morning as he sent those fellows in afterwards, Sundquist. And he said,"You know, I used to be a saloon keeper. I kept a saloon for six years.And all the nasty answers that a man can make, I've heard them there and I remember them."(laughs)This Sundquist was doing something there and Watson come along and he wanted to talk to him, he had his teeth out and he had them in his hands.Sundquist looked at him."That old son of a bitch has his teeth in his hands ready to bite somebody."(laughs) Watson says, "Good god, I didn't think he'd say a, thing like that to me.""Say anythint to you, you old son of a bitch." Boy he was just nipping to get away. This guy got a under him, he'd just ride him a man that he could ride he'd ride him, but if he didn't and he wanted him there, why he was alright. He was quite a Scotchman. He was a brickmaker alright.

speaker: SS
words: What about the bank of Troy? Do you know the early days , how they made out?

speaker: FR
words: I don't know. There was two of them there. There used to be one where the post office is too. In the early days, I guess they got along alright. I never knew of any trouble.Take one of those, head of the bank across the street at one time where the post office is, his wife worked in there. I don't know, the bank sold out, that's how they quit it. Mf*. o£ckA taught school there a while. A fellow one time, he wanted to garnishee somebody else, he was av of Jesse Spencer, he wanted to garnishee somebody's bank account, it happened he owed him some money. Well he says,Mrs. Beckman was cashier there at the time. He says,"I thought I knew I couldn't get the information out of him, I thought I could get it out of her.I asked her, so and so got a bank account here?" Says, you present these checks for payment and find out."(laughs)He wasn't so good about it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember Ole Bohman very much?

speaker: FR
words: Oh yes, knew him same as I knew my dad or my brother.You bet, fine fellow too. And big Axel, his brother, he was like O.K. Olson. He was a cutup, he'd laugh, big Axel. Ole was quieter. This, you heard of Brocke, how he extended the business. Well now,he didn't originate that. I suppose Axel more likely did. I believe Axel, he was more enterprising than Ole was. But Frank jus extended it, really went to work on it,

speaker: SS
words: He was trained by Ole, wasn't he?

speaker: FR
words: 01 yes, he just came in there, high school guy, you know, young fella.

speaker: LR
words: Course, we haven't seen him for a long time, but do your remember him when he first come there, he was just a kid. And now he's getting to be an old man. Been sick and then this bank deal,they kicked him out of the bank.It's been a terrible hard thing on him.

speaker: FR
words: He'll soon be able to take a hand in that bank in Juliaetta when it gets there.

speaker: LR
words: Didn't you read about that the other day?It looks like they're not going to get it. They just don't think it would go there. So the guy said they're going to try to raise more money and then see how it went.

speaker: FR
words: Frank told they expected the charter any day. That is,to have this university lawyer...

speaker: LR
words: Oh yes, but this is a...(discussion about the bank)

speaker: FR
words: Figured it would detract from the business, they did mention Lewiston, but they mentioned Genesee and Troy. But hell, they deserve a bank down in that part of the country.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think that the bank's policies helped to keep Troy going during the depression? Do you think it did much for the town in the hard days?

speaker: FR
words: Well, at this point I don't know what they done. I never heard nothing for or against it, but I know this much about it: they done all they could do. Because if Frank wasn't doing it Ole was or Axel. And the three of them were that kind of men. As an example of what Frank done: One spring there was a fellow working on the WPA there, I was a foreman, I had a WPA gang because I was highway foreman. And we had a fellow there, Frank Hedrick working on it and the son of a gun was no good to work. But his brother was shot down in Ontario,Oregon and :. up in a shack, they say. Shack burnt. And he wanted to go down there and look on to his brother's interest down there. He thought he probably had something he might get. Since he coooldn't He went to Ole and told him what he wanted and wanted to know if he could get fifty dollars. Now mind you, he was on felief. He didn't have a good job. And Ole let him have it.No security. So you see,Brocke had got nothing on what Ole had. And I know darn well that Axel was as good a man as either one. As friendly and as generous a man.

speaker: LR
words: Well Brocke, he followed along something like that too, I guess. The time the bank was, the first bank holdup down there, not too many years after Frank loaned moeny to this guy. Frank was the one, the guy come up to the window to the girl there to give him the money and scared her so she fainted.He told Frank to come up and give him the money. So had to come up and hand him the money and he went and got away. They caught him before too long. And he must have went to prison for a while. Anyway, m while afterwards, he come to the bank to borrow some money.

speaker: FR
words: After he done his time in the penitentury.,.

speaker: LR
words: Frank let him have it.

speaker: FR
words: Well you know, he shouldn't have let him have it.For this reason. His judgement was damn poor. That man had never been caught if he halfway attended to business. He goes right to Spokane ^ trader his car in for eight hundred dollars on a Dodge car. Well he lays around, he was a no good bum around over in Fernwood over there. He'd pay a bill here and he'd pay a bill there.

speaker: LR
words: Buy this and that.

speaker: FR
words: Just jokingly a guy was talking to him said,"Where in hell did you get the money?" A Just a joke. Fellow says,"By god, maybe he held up the bank of Troy. If he'd just kept on going like he was, you know,salted that money, he was alright.

speaker: SS
words: Did he admit it right there?

speaker: FR
words: They picked him up,and they got a lot of it in the back of his car, a lot of the money was back there. I guess he didn't deny it, I don't know, But he turned himself in, that's all.

speaker: LR
words: He com^ in with his dinner bucket, just like the working men come into the bank for a little business, you know, which he did. He putAdinner bucket they had up there and told the girl to put all the money^in it and she fainted. And then told Frank to come up and fill it. And he beat it out and nobody igot could the car number, couldn't describe him enough figure out who he was. And the way they caught him up there at Fernwood, just like abunch of 'em would be standing out here in the yard and or else sitting here talking and,"Where in the world did he get that money?" "Well, maybe he's the one that robbed the Troy bank."And some others said,"Say this joking, but it might not be such ajoke. That just might be what happened."So whatever they did after that, we don't remember, but anyway, they went on that and they caught him.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hear aboi* that guy that paid to have his saloon burned down? In Troy. Henning was the guy, he paid the guy to have his saloon burned down.

speaker: FR
words: Oh yeah, that was wati back too. That corner building,it burnt there. Around where,on'5 store is now.Somebody hired somebody to burn that up. That was another thing that Steele brought up after Mallock was shot in Grangeville, Two or three years after he burnt that building, he confessed to it that he burned the building, he was outjfes marshall in Culdesac.(laughs) That's how things went in them days.

speaker: LR
words: See how many trusting people fchere were at the same time there were crooks? Oh boy, things like that wouldn't go today. But they had quite a lot about this guy. He made Frank put the money in the dinnerbucket for him and and then he comes back and borrows money from him.

speaker: SS
words: That is funny. I suppose Frank would be one of the very few people that would loan money.

speaker: LR
words: They said, "That take Frank Brocke, he'd only one'd do it." And he got it back too, they said. The guy paid it back.

speaker: SS
words: He's an awfully good hearted man.

speaker: LR
words: Oh yes, he's a nice man.

speaker: FR
words: In Silverton, I believe they call it, there was hammers going all over town, railroad coming in. Funny thing too, they made a contract to let 20 miles down the river from there. You come in on the trail on the train about 2 o'clock in the afternoon. The mail come up in about five in the evening. This contract hadn't been... But I was in the barbershop that trip in or I was going into the Interior homestead, and when I got in there I wouldn't think the whole damn county had got out there to homestead. I was in there in the barbershop, fella come in there, I guess it was the marshall was in the chair,He come in there and the marshall'd do something, was going to arrest somebody. He pulled a gun outAthere. And he was so damn shaky and that big gun he had made me kind of shaky too.(laughs) After while then they talked the barber in and the marshall talked to him, by god, he was going to make the marshall go

speaker: SS
words: Did you have any run ins with the IWWs?

speaker: FR
words: Oh, they were just a regular run of lumberjacks there. They had, they was supposed to be a union the company started, a union and you had to join it to work in the woods. I wasn't in the woods then. I farmed there. But they had this big IWW, you paid a dollar andAyou was a union man. They had them in there. They took quite a lot of fellows that wouldn't join, they had a bullpen up here someplace, I don't know where it was. We lived on the creek there on the main road between Troy and Deary. And see them going through, ten, fifteen men in trucks hollering.(laughs) They wasn't excited. There was one fellow there used to go around, a bucker, bucker's a man;.that saws alone. He didn't want to work with anybody. He'd carry his falling board with him. He carried a horseshoe affair that made, nail it on here and on the cork and when you cut a notch tfnto your tree, there'd be like that. And they take this springboard and get up on it and saw your tree. Takes a little practice to do it, like riding a bicycle. But he had to go to work for the company, there was no gyppoing, they had to go to work by the day. He said,"Well I knew what was what. Who went out," And T.P. Jones come along, superintendent there. T.P. comes along ty where he was working and says,"What is this kept down 4-Ls ,pay anyway?" Or what does this job pay?" Jones was alittle out of humor. "The 4-Ls schedule is on the door there in my office."To hell with the 4-Ls, I'm working for the government! (laughs) "Go down the road, you're a cranky son of a bitch! We don't want you around here."That's what he wanted, to get fired. Then he could gyppo. 4-Ls schedule's on the door. So there was a little excitement with them. Didn't amount to much, nobody took it very serious. Never got nowhere's with it and they^do away with the blankets and got the eight hour day. Wobblies done that. It was ten hours and pack your own blankets before that.Another thing required was to keep the hogs penned up around camps.They wanted the blankets furnished and sheets on the beds.

speaker: SS
words: So they did something good?

speaker: FR
words: Oh you bet your life they did.He give them tough, and they were just sort of rough lumberjacks. Nobody was afraid of them.

speaker: LR
words: Anything went up in smoke, why, that was the Wobblies, wasn't it?

speaker: FR
words: Yeah.Harrison, you know.

speaker: LR
words: Was that the same thing?

speaker: FR
words: Just happened to come to town the day the fire in Harrison. What were they hollering?"Send us out the militia, the Wobblies burnt up our town. I" 'The Wobblies didn't burn your town, but here's a hundred dollars, start a relief. They happened to be right there in town at the time. Come across the lake on the boat, you see. The govenor had a hundred bucks? Govenor of Idaho was there at the time. Did he get, was it the govenor gave a hundred bucks? Hundred bucks to start a relief. He blamed the Wobblies? Oh no, he told those people he knew it wasn't the Wobblies, So they pinned a lot on the Wobblies that they never done? Oh sure. They had them in here, arresting men from Camp Six, fellas, one old man was in there at the time he was said they came in there and he jumped up and stuck his hands up in the air.(laughs)Says, this officer in charge says,"That's alright, that's alright. Put your hands down and sit down."That man's got a gun. I'm scared!" he said.(laughs) He was making fun of them, you know,"That man's got a gun, I'm scared!"

And he'd come in there and lecture one Sunday to it. But he couldn't even talk. Talking about something about being careful,"Or you might get hurted," he says. He was preaching around, I don't know what in the hell, whoever got him on the move, start him in to that. didn't have a hell of a lot to do. There's a Dick Ferrell, there's a minister, I see him mentioned in the Lewiston paper the other day, I was wondering if that damn fool was still alive,

"Well," he says,"the Diamond Match." Now the food too, depends on the cooking push. If the cook was any good, they were not always good. I cooked in the camp myself. I know.(laughs)

words: Yes.

words: (kiddingly)That's enough out of you!

And this blacksmith there, he was. cookjjat Camp Ten, he had these damn little shacks to cook for.He didn't board.(laughs)So the blacksmith, he was in the kitchen there too, there was probably a half a dozen of us in there. "By god so and so," he'd called him a name,"I don't see any of the crewel used to cook for. You know, you, I didn't cook for you didn't live there." "By god none of theT did!"(laughter)

Well he didn't do too good a job cleaning them birds. The guy cooked 'em,(laughs) And I don't know Who if One fellow said to the other,"Did you notice that goddamn cook didn't, that goddamn cook cooked up them 'buds'? He didn't finish cleaning 'em that we left."(laughs)"I told 'em to clean 'em, they said they would, so they slapped 'em in there."

words: No wonder there was a lot of dead men in there.

speaker: FR
words: That time I was cooking there. He had a camp above us. And they had a pet bear there. Had him on a chain. You know,you can take them bears, be just as playful and full of fun as any pup you've ever seen. And you five minutes, he's out in a fight. And Gil used to get along with him pretty good.He'd attack Gil and he tore the pants off of him.(laughs) They organized a funeral for that bear then. Fellow done that one time in Troy in the barbershop. Right beside where the bank is today. He had this cub there, this fella got two of 'em, Tom Christie that buried the stiff in the canyon, he took the other bear and the police come around, before he skaddeled, come around the West coast. There was a big of people crowded there from all directions see the fleet. And Tom, he take that other bear up and he give this to the fleet for a mascot there.

speaker: FR
words: This barber had one in the woodshed then and ther about that high and he'd go out there and wrastle with the bear. He get up on the post and grab the kid and get along fine,Then his wife went out there one day and she was, about their first child was about to come home s*nd she went out to where the bear was and he jumped at her and she fell over a salt block there and golly, he come out there and he just took the old bear and strung him over the door.(laughs) Chain wasn't long enough to let him on the ground so he hung to death. That settled that bear's hash. But it was a lot a fun when they was kidding the bear right when they're...

speaker: LR
words: That was like this place we were working at when we met, my aunt and her husband, they had a goat.A billy goat. And they also had these, well I guess maybe the youngest girl wasn't around yet. But the twins they were walking and out around, climbing the fence, one thing and another.

speaker: LR
words: Course, they had the yard fenced in, but the kids, they'd go out and climb up on the fence and the billy would come up there and bytt and walk around there and butt, you know and couldn't get to them to get to them, that was fun.So we was mentioning it to him that he better get rid of that goat and sometime those kids might get outside he vtfould get to them. And I was afraid to go across. When I went up to get the mail, I tell you, I looked to see where that goat was. And I kept looking back to see where he was. But he never bothered Bill. And whenever he would get in the way, Bill just with his foot just shoo him and kick him away,"And get out of the way Billy, get away." So one morning, Bill was coming in from milking and he had a pail in each hand and coming from the barn with the milk. And Billy tore into him. I don't know whether from behind or front or how it was.

speaker: FR
words: He stuck his horns right into his...

speaker: LR
words: So Bill, he just sets his milk down there, goes in, takes out his pocketknife and cuts Mr. Goat's throat. That was the end of that.(laughs)

speaker: FR
words: He said,"By god, I'll buy my chaps." Used to say he was going to raise a nice pair of chaps there. He said,"By god,I'11 buy my chaps."(laughs)

speaker: LR
words: He said,"I don't want him sticking any horns into my kids." Or anybody else. Just calmly took out his knife and cut his throat.

speaker: FR
words: You could bang that goat all you wanted with rocks, sticks or anything. He'd still stay with you. The only way you could get a yelp out of him was if you got him by the hair and pull two ways, just close together.He'd baaaa. The first ones to work there, the grainery door was off the hinges...

speaker: FR
words: See, this horn catch. And turn right down, him in the face. He seen all he wanted I guess.

speaker: SS
words: This was where you were sleeping? That same goat that he cut his throat. He was going to raise himself a pair of chaps. It was a nice goat. But when it horned him, he didn't like it.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have much run in with wildlife when you were out by Troy? Was there deer around there?

speaker: FR
words: I didn't see many. She did. What did you see, seven in a bunch one time, wasn't it?

speaker: LR
words: Yeah.

speaker: FR
words: Just, from the house.

speaker: LR
words: Just a little ways out from the house. Well, about as far as from here out to the house across the street. They were^kitty cornered, like they went in from here,going across. And there were seven of them old ones and babies like, you know. And they there and they horned each other and they'd run, they buck just like cattle out in the pasture. And I was so afraid the dog would see 'em and make a fuss.And I wanted to watch them. So dog was out the back and he didn't seem to sniff them or hear them or anything. And I kept quiet so he wouldn't be coming around the front to where I might be. And they just took their time going by, out through there and running and butting and going on. They come to the division in the field there, the big ones,+hey just hop over that just like we put a ruler down on the floor and step over it. The little ones, they'd go through the fence and went on across. But then another time I looked way over across the other way on the Big Meadow, over in the Big Meadow country to be and going up the line, they were going up, looked like right on the top of the hill.If they'd been-down the hill a little ways I wouldn't have seen them. But they was up enough so's you could see them. And I think it was eleven or twelve. They just kept acomin and kept acoming. Going across from one woods to the other, you see. So there used to be lots of deer down there.

speaker: FR
words: Another thing I saw there,...

speaker: LR
words: I dnn't know how they are now, I don't suppose they're so plentiful.

speaker: FR
words: Back on that Ceme tery road, back of the place we had there was a big white cat, I suppose was Tom's putting in the back of the woods there. And saw that cat go up Cemetery road and the damn deer probably 50,60 feet behind it. Both walking along easy. After while the deer come back and the cat come back.

speaker: LR
words: They were friends back there, you see.

speaker: FR
words: Another morning though, I suppose it was nine or ten o'clock in the daytime, happened to be the day I was home. Seen an old deer and her calf over pretty much between us and Johnson's in our field. And the calf laid down there and it went on.So by god, we were going to watch and see her when evening come. She come back That thing never showed up. Went all the way to maybe a half an hour or so until she got hungry, and it was running around and jump up, and baaaa. Jump in the wheat, jump up to look around. Stood there and finally after while she come along and seen him way over at Johnson's.Calf was looking around.

speaker: LR
words: she was calling for him. She'd blatt a little.

speaker: FR
words: So the calf come up and there on the run. And she stopped right there and it was dinnertime for that calf.

speaker: LR
words: Bo you know these black angus cattle?Coal black you know and look like a bear? And especially if they're half grown or so. Do you know the Orvil Carlson was one, over in the Big Meadow, over there, back of Troy? He had, I don't know if he had more than one or not, but he had a black angus steer. And so one evening, it was pretty near dark, and the neighbor come, knocked on the door. Orvil went to the door, and the guys says to him','0rvil, how much will you take for that black angus steer?" And Orvil laughed and he said, "What's the matter, did you shoot a bear?" And that's just what he done, he shot that angus thinking it was a bear.(laughs)So Carlson put his price on it and the guy paid it, it was alright. But just that quick, he laughed and he said,"What's the matter?Did you kill a bear?" "He said, "Yeah, I was over there right up against the timber, I thought it was a bear."" Well, I can understand that. It looks like a bear in the daytime."(laughs)

speaker: FR
words: We had a bear show up at the backend of the house. We seen 'em back in the field and then a farmer come in there with his combine one day. "This country's getting to be sort of a national park, isn't it? I've run into bear and deer today." That was Henry Hanson, lives out here, beyond town. .

speaker: LR
words: You forgot about the time the bear co/tje up to our chicken house after those fat scraps. I couldn't sleep, it was about 2 or 2:30, IAcould not sleep. And it was summertime, warm you know.And I had on my nightgown. I thought, wll, I wouldn't get cold or anything, I'd just slip into my shoes and go out and walk around in the yard and around the house there, maybe I'd get sleepy after a while.Well, I still wasn't sleepy and I thought if I went back to bed, that probably keep him awake and he needed his sleep to get up and go to work, I just laid down on the davenport and the davenport was right across the window. And so I hadn't been laying down there but a very few minutes -hi wham! bang! I couldn't imagine what in the world was that.So I thought, we had pans and things to feed the do«s and cats in. The dog and the cats. And I thought, well, we didn't have a dog at that time. But I though well it's something, tin somethings up on the porch and my sister's dog had come up across there, cause we lived right up on the hill from Spencer's down there. I thought it was Rex up there and I was going to aet up and see what it, So I didn't get up so whatever it was would see me through the window, right in the line of two windows, see. They could see anybody in there.

speaker: LR
words: So I just rolled off of the davenport and crawled around to get between the window and I got up to the glass door and got my finder on the switch there so I could turn it on real quick and they see me and I see what it was. So I flipped it on, not a darn thing out there! I looked all around. I was afraid to go out at first and I looked^and then I opened the door and I looked out, not a thing! Well I didn't know what it was. I still thought maybe it was Rex. Was up andknocked something around. So I went back to bed, laid down on the davenport again. That was 3 o'clock then. But the next day I guessFrank found it, the next morning I guess when he was up and went out there, wanted chickens or something. And there was that, we'd butchered. We'd raised, we bought little pigs and raised them for our meet. And I had a lot more fat and lard and stuff than we could use, so I'd make soap out of it. So get it away from the house and out, why, I took it out there and put it down on some tins. And an old double boiler, over top of it and got it all in and put the double boiler over it. But I didn't weight it out, it wouldn't make any difference to the bear. But if anybody walked on there, those tins were about that long and about that wide. They just placed around on each other and they'd hit each other and that's what made that noise. That bear after that lard stuff under there.

speaker: SS
words: How did you know it was a bear.

speaker: LR
words: Well it just couldn't have been anything else. That could do that«But when I switched the light on, why then of course, it run. Because we had seen it different times way over close to Spencer's, just right up back of the Spencer son down there, of the two, one the road from the other. Against the woods there. So it had to be a bear.

words: She couldn't talk English. But she was a goodhearted, jolly, old girl. Well about all I can tell about her,she wouldn't let me correct my kid there one time. Our oldest girl, she was just walking good. And Grandma.had cookies made. So she was going to be good to the baby and she wasn't fuss. But she wanted to give her a treat. So she come, takes he;r by the hand, leads her off, that was in the front room. I guess it was brown sugar wasn't it?
